ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002694 covers fried rice as a food product category; no specific Indian-style veg/egg/chicken fried rice entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers fried rice generically; no variant-specific code for Indian-style veg/egg/chicken fried rice. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for fried rice as a prepared dish food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Indian-style fried rice is approximately 300 g (one plate), comprising stir-fried rice with egg, chicken pieces, and mixed vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, Chicken — HPB Singapore Foodo / ICMR-NIN adapted composite
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo provides nutrient data for chicken fried rice (Singapore context). Values for the veg/egg/chicken composite variant are adapted from HPB Foodo chicken fried rice entry and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN cooked rice and egg data. The composite represents a mid-point between vegetable-only and chicken variants. Per-100g values reflect a typical mixed preparation as served in Singapore Indian restaurants.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice
gi_source.source_match_notes: University of Sydney GI Database reports GI of approximately 54 for white fried rice (cooked, stir-fried). This value is used as the closest analogous estimate for Indian-style veg/egg/chicken fried rice, which shares the same base ingredient (white rice, stir-fried). GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 73.5) / 100 ≈ 39.7, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 24.5 × 3 = 73.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1140 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-salt preparation, as a single 300 g serving provides approximately 1140 mg sodium — nearly 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 54) and high glycemic load per serving (~39.7)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of fried rice is in the low range (54), the large carbohydrate load per standard serving (~73.5 g) produces a high glycemic load of approximately 39.7; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce portion size to half a plate and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium; cooking oil quantity variable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate saturated fat (~1.1 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium (~380 mg per 100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose smaller portions, request less oil during preparation, and monitor overall daily sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Indian-style fried rice (vegetable, egg, and chicken variant) is a popular stir-fried rice dish served across Singapore's Indian restaurants and hawker stalls. It is prepared by stir-frying pre-cooked white rice in a wok with vegetable oil, beaten eggs, diced chicken, mixed vegetables (such as carrots, peas, and corn), onion, garlic, soy sauce, and seasoning. The dish has a savoury, slightly smoky flavour from wok-frying (wok hei), a tender yet slightly chewy texture from the rice, and a satisfying combination of protein from egg and chicken with carbohydrate from rice. It is commonly served as a standalone meal or alongside curries and raita. Nutritionally, a 300 g serving provides approximately 516 kcal, 20 g protein, 15.6 g fat, and 73.5 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is high (~1140 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is approximately 54 (low range), but the glycemic load per serving is high (~39.7) due to the large carbohydrate portion, which is clinically relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is generally halal-compliant when prepared with halal-certified chicken and contains egg and soy allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried rice?
Is fried rice good for diabetics?
Is Indian fried rice high in sodium?
What is the GI of fried rice?
How much protein is in egg fried rice?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo Singapore chicken fried rice data and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN cooked rice and egg entries. No single approved source provides an exact match for the Indian-style veg/egg/chicken composite variant as served at this restaurant. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white fried rice. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent a composite estimate for the veg/egg/chicken fried rice variant, adapted from HPB Foodo (chicken fried rice, Singapore) and ICMR-NIN data for cooked rice and egg. Exact restaurant recipe is unknown. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN source entries for this dish. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN source entries for this dish. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN source entries for this dish. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in vegetable-oil-based stir-fry preparations per HPB Foodo data. 6. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white fried rice; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). 7. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Grains and staples' as rice is the dominant macronutrient contributor. 8. Halal status assumes halal-certified chicken; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 9. The cuisine tag 'Butter Chicken' in the input appears to be a field survey platform artefact and has not been carried forward as it does not describe this dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_group from 'Meat and alternatives' (input) to 'Grains and staples' (rice-dominant dish).

Populated full per_100g nutrient profile from HPB Foodo / ICMR-NIN adapted composite.
Added GI of 54 from University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate for white fried rice) with gi_category 'low'.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit source-absence justification in notes.
Ensured all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in flat aliases list by adding 'Chao Fan (Shucai / Jidan / Jirou)' to the flat aliases array.
Added health_context for all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Set ontology codes as closest_parent with gap notes; no codes invented.
